topic: A receipt from a Sears store that closed in two thousand eighteen fell out of the lining of a winter coat I bought online last month and every item on the receipt is something currently in my house
title: A Receipt from a Sears That Closed in Twenty Eighteen Fell Out of a Coat I Bought Last Month. The Items Are All Mine. | True Scary Story
description_intro:
A folded thermal receipt fell out of the inner lining of a brand new winter coat I ordered from REI last month. The receipt was dated November fourteenth, two thousand eighteen, from a Sears store. Sears closed every location in my state in October of two thousand eighteen. The receipt lists twelve items. Every single one of them is in my house right now.

The cardholder line on the receipt matches a Visa I cancelled in two thousand nineteen. The signature is mine. The total is the exact amount that was charged to my current card last month.
description:
A folded thermal receipt fell out of the inner lining of a brand new winter coat I ordered from REI last month. The receipt was dated November fourteenth, two thousand eighteen, from a Sears store. Sears closed every location in my state in October of two thousand eighteen. The receipt lists twelve items. Every single one of them is in my house right now.

The cardholder line on the receipt matches a Visa I cancelled in two thousand nineteen. The signature is mine. The total is the exact amount that was charged to my current card last month for an order I never placed. The address printed on the receipt is now a U-Haul rental lot. The manager pulled their security footage as a favor. The man on the tape was wearing the same coat I had just cut open on my kitchen counter.

The second coat arrived four days later. I had not ordered it. The receipt sewn into the lining of the second coat is dated for next year. The items listed are things I have only been thinking about buying. Watch to the end. The cardholder signature on the new receipt is also mine.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I ordered a winter coat from REI last month. It arrived in a cardboard box on a Tuesday with the factory tags still attached to the sleeve. I wore it once during a snowstorm the next weekend and hung it on the back of a kitchen chair to dry. While I was waiting for the wool to stop dripping I ran my hand down the inside of the coat. There was a small rectangle of paper folded inside the lining at the lower seam, near my left hip. I could feel the edges through the wool. I cut the lining open with a pair of kitchen shears. The paper inside was a folded thermal receipt from a Sears store. The receipt was dated November fourteenth, two thousand eighteen. Sears closed every location in my state in October of two thousand eighteen.

close detail of factory tags still attached to the sleeve of a new winter coat, a price tag dangling from a small plastic loop, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a hand running along the inner lining of a coat at the lower hip seam, only the hand visible, dim kitchen light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a folded thermal receipt resting on a kitchen counter beside a pair of open kitchen shears, the paper slightly curled, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I bought the coat online from the REI website on a Saturday morning. It was a discounted winter parka in a dark navy color, size large. The website listed it as new in stock and shipped from a warehouse in Kent, Washington. The order confirmation email gave me a tracking number that I followed for three days. The package was scanned at four sorting facilities and delivered to my porch by a regional carrier. The box arrived sealed with REI branded tape. The plastic bag inside was unopened. The coat itself had the factory tags still attached at the sleeve and the collar. The hangtag listed a serial number and a country of manufacture. There was no indication anywhere on the packaging that the coat had been previously owned. It was not a return. It was not a resale. The coat was new.

wide exterior of a covered residential porch with a single sealed shipping box on the welcome mat, afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of REI branded packing tape across the seam of a cardboard box, the lettering crisp and unbroken, horizontal 16:9 framing

close detail of a paper hangtag on the collar of a new winter coat showing a serial number and a small barcode, single overhead bulb, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I do laundry on Sundays. It is a routine I have kept since I lived alone in my twenties. I sort the loads in the basement before lunch and hang anything wool to dry on a wooden rack near the furnace. The morning after the snowstorm I carried the new coat downstairs to brush off the salt and let it air. That is when I noticed the rectangle again. The paper was lower in the lining than I had thought, almost behind the hem. I carried the coat back upstairs and laid it flat across the kitchen island under the overhead bulb. I held the wool taut with one hand and cut a four inch slit along the inside seam with kitchen shears. The receipt slid out on its own. It was warm in my palm. The thermal paper was not yellowed.

wide overhead shot of a kitchen island with a dark coat laid flat under a single overhead bulb, no figure, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of kitchen shears cutting a slit along the inside seam of a coat lining, only the shears and the fabric visible, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a folded thermal receipt resting in the palm of a hand beside the cut lining of a coat, dim kitchen light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I unfolded the receipt on the counter and flattened the creases with my thumb. The header at the top read Sears, with a store number and the address of a location in the next county. The transaction date was printed below the header. November fourteenth, two thousand eighteen. The receipt listed twelve items. I read the first line out loud without thinking. Craftsman twelve inch adjustable wrench. I knew that wrench. I had the exact wrench in my garage on a pegboard above my workbench. I had bought it new at a hardware store two years ago, in two thousand twenty three. I walked out to the garage with the receipt in my hand. The wrench was on its hook. I lifted it down and turned it over. The small silver Craftsman serial sticker on the handle matched the partial number printed on the receipt.
image_prompts: close shot of a hand flattening a folded thermal receipt on a kitchen counter, only the hand and the paper visible, single overhead bulb, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of the top of a thermal receipt showing the word Sears and a small store address line, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a Craftsman twelve inch adjustable wrench hanging on a pegboard hook above a workbench, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a small silver Craftsman serial sticker on the handle of an adjustable wrench, the digits partially worn, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I told myself it was a coincidence. Sears had sold millions of Craftsman wrenches over its lifetime. The serial sticker numbers were probably batch identifiers shared across an entire production run. The receipt itself was a piece of trash that had been sewn into the lining as packing filler or had fallen in by accident at a warehouse. The date might be a printer error. The store might have reopened briefly under a liquidation lease. I folded the receipt and put it on top of the microwave next to the spare key dish. I made coffee. I did not read the rest of the line items. I went upstairs to fold the laundry I had brought up from the basement. The wool coat was still drying on the kitchen chair. The cut lining hung open against my leg as I passed it.

close detail of a coffee maker mid brew on a kitchen counter, steam rising from the carafe, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of folded laundry on top of a bed in a quiet bedroom, single overhead bulb, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I read the rest of the receipt the next morning with a cup of coffee at the kitchen island. Item two was a Kenmore microwave, model E two C. There was a Kenmore E two C in my kitchen ten feet from where I was sitting. Item three was a Lands End fleece pullover in steel blue, size large. I was wearing that fleece at that moment. Item four was a Schwinn twenty four inch girls bike in purple. My daughter's bike was in the garage. Item five was a glass casserole dish with a green lid. It was on my counter beside the stove. I read all twelve items. Every single line was something currently in my house. The brands matched. The sizes matched. The colors matched. The model numbers matched. The receipt described, item by item, a small inventory of my own home.
image_prompts: close shot of a hand holding a thermal receipt above a kitchen counter, a coffee mug beside it, single overhead bulb, horizontal 16:9 framing

close detail of a glass casserole dish with a green lid sitting on a kitchen counter beside a stovetop, dim afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I turned the receipt over and read the payment block at the bottom. The tender line said Visa. The last four digits were four four two nine. I knew that number. Four four two nine had been the last four digits of a Visa card I cancelled in two thousand nineteen after the issuer was bought by another bank. The receipt was dated one year before I had cancelled the card. That part lined up. The signature line on the bottom of the receipt was printed in thin gray ink. The signature was mine. The forward slant of the capital letter at the start. The closed loop on the third letter of my last name. The small upturn on the final stroke that I have used since high school. I held the receipt up to the window light and compared it to the signature on my driver's license.
image_prompts: close shot of a thermal receipt flipped to show the payment block with the word Visa printed at the top of the section, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a hand holding a thermal receipt up against the light of a kitchen window, afternoon light filtering through, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a driver's license lying on a kitchen counter next to a thermal receipt with the signature panels aligned, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a printed signature on a thermal receipt, the slant of the letters clearly visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The total at the bottom of the receipt was one thousand one hundred fourteen dollars and twenty seven cents. I carried the receipt to the office and opened the file cabinet where I keep old credit card statements. I found the binder for two thousand eighteen and pulled the November statement. I read every line item. There was no Sears charge of any amount on the statement. The card had been used at a grocery store, a gas station, and an auto repair shop that month. I almost closed the binder. Then I went back to my desk and opened my current credit card account online. I scrolled to November of last month. There was a single charge that I had not been able to place at the time. One thousand one hundred fourteen dollars and twenty seven cents. Sears. Dated November fourteenth, two thousand twenty five.
image_prompts: close detail of the total line on a thermal receipt showing a dollar amount printed in large characters, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a laptop screen showing a current credit card transaction list with a single highlighted entry from a closed store, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I drove to the address printed on the receipt the following Saturday. Three three zero zero West Belt Line Road. The building was a long beige rectangle set back from the road behind a chain link fence. The sign above the office door read U-Haul. The man at the counter told me the property had been a U-Haul rental lot since two thousand nineteen. I told him about the receipt. He pulled the security footage for November fourteenth of last month as a favor. We watched the front camera together. At eight twenty three in the evening a man in a dark navy REI parka walked out of the office carrying twelve white plastic bags. The bags were heavy. The timestamp on the footage matched the time printed on the receipt. I had been at home with my wife at eight twenty three that night.

sections.narration: I drove home and threw the coat into the trash. Four days later another REI box arrived on the porch. I had not placed an order. The tracking email said it was a replacement for an exchanged item. The replacement was a new winter coat. Same brand. Same size. Same dark navy color. I cut the lining open with the same shears. There was a folded thermal receipt sewn into the same lower seam. The date at the top was November fourteenth, two thousand twenty six. Sears, three three zero zero West Belt Line Road. The twelve items on the new receipt were not things I owned. They were things I had been quietly thinking about buying. The cardholder signature was mine. The total was one thousand one hundred fourteen dollars and twenty seven cents.
